speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four of our interview with Esther Clifford. We were talking about your trying to get help to get out of Germany and the agency you went to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, I went to the agency in this [NON-ENGLISH] in Frankfurt. And they were able to get me a visa that would allow me to get into England. It was a domestic visa. England needed a lot of domestic helpers at the time, and that was the only way to get to England. I was too old for the children's group. There were children being sent to England, but I was not young enough anymore. And so I was able to get a visa from them. I have-- had lived now with this Hahn family that I had talked about earlier, and the daughter, my friend, had a visa, also a domestic visa, to go to England. And the parents were very reluctant to-- to send their daughter to England on their own, because they were awaiting that family visa, and they wanted to go together. Also, this family thought that the visa I had, I would never get into England. I don't remember now what it was, but there was something missing, and they claimed that this visa would not bring me to England, and it was also dangerous to go to England with this visa because my name was on it, and I was supposed to be deported to Poland. In order for her daughter not to go-- the daughter wanted very much to go to England, but as I mentioned before, the parents didn't want to. And I believe it was in order for her to hold her back. They-- but the mother did. She changed the name of that visa to my name and some kind of other dates that she-- she changed it because she-- she said that I would never make it with my-- with my visa, and she was afraid that they were going to keep me on the border and-- and send me someplace. So she said I should take her daughter's visa, and then if that doesn't help, I can always go and show them my visa that was given to me. But she said it was a-- a more proper visa to go to England. And-- And they wanted me to promise that I would-- once I got there, I would go to their relatives, and urge them, and tell them what was going on in Germany and to hurry up with the family visa that they were waiting for. I took this visa, and I made it to England. In England, I was-- I-- I was waiting for someone to pick me up. I had no one, but I was told that in England, there are people that had-- it's called Woburn House, W-O-B-U-R-N. They were waiting for refugees to come over, and they were waiting there at the railway station. But I waited a day and a night in the railway station, and I couldn't see anyone. I really had no one. But then, the next day, somebody did pick me up, and she took me to a shelter. It was shelter that had about 50 to 100 cots in there. And in this shelter, people came to look over the young girls that had come over to do domestic help. Because I was always thin and small, nobody really wanted me as domestic help. So I was the only one left, but then a English lady came, and she took anyone who she could get hold of, but it was not London, it was Brighton. It was-- at the time, I didn't know where that was. It was about an hour away. And I worked for these people. I was waiting about nine months before I was able to get to the people that-- to these relatives of my friend, Hertha Hahn, and-- in order to-- to talk to them about the-- the situation in Germany. And I urged them to do whatever they can to get them out, and they said they would. But meanwhile, war had broken out, and that place where I worked in Brighton did not allow any foreigners to be there, because it was by the ocean. It was by the ocean, and the German bombs, the German airplanes came-- started coming over, bombing, and the people didn't want to have a German in their house. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so that was my experience in England. I worked as a domestic and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the Hahn family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hahn family perished. None of them have ever gotten out. That was very hard for me to take, but there were-- after the war, I found out that-- that they were-- they were-- they had been taken. They were deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in contact with anybody in your family during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in contact with mostly my-- my sister in France. Before-- before France was overrun by Hitler, we were able to-- to correspond. But once it was overrun, they had-- they fled from one place to another, and they ended up in the-- near Vichy, near the Vichy government. And then they went-- and until then, I was able to-- we were in touch. Not often, but a few times. And it was my sister in France who had-- who had received a couple of cards from Warsaw, from my parents. And she sent me those cards, where they said that they were starving. They have nothing to eat, and that we have to help them. And my sister said that she sent them a couple of packages. She doesn't know whether they ever received them. By that time, they were in a ghetto, in the Warsaw ghetto. And-- and from then on, I was-- was always very concerned, but I worried myself sick. It was-- I was always thinking about them. And while in England, we heard so many things, that-- that people had been deported. And they-- and we heard about concentration camps and that people were starving. And I was always very, very concerned, so concerned that I was ill many times, just because of my concern for my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date of the-- the cards that you got from Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one card sent, I remember, in-- there were only two cards, and one was sent in the end of 1940, and another card we-- I received at the-- in '40-- 1941. And after that, we didn't hear anything more from them. But they did say that they were in the ghetto, and they had-- they were-- they were starving. They had nothing to eat. They mentioned at one time that my brother lived with a family, and they gave an address from-- not [INAUDIBLE]. The second card said that if you want to write to Leo, his address is such and such. I don't know what that meant. We did write, but we never heard anything from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in England, after you left Brighton, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to London. And I went gladly to London because in Brighton, I had no one to talk to. I-- First of-- I-- I-- in Brighton, there was no one-- no refugees. We were considered refugees at the time. And what I did in Brighton is learned my English. As a matter of fact, there's-- I-- I learned-- I-- I worked very hard. They-- these people made me work very hard. And I would wear a little apron and such, and I always kept a little book in my pocket of my apron because I wanted to learn English. And whenever I heard a word that I didn't know, I would write it down, and then at night, in bed, I had a flashlight. Under the blankets, I would study my English because I wasn't allowed to-- to have any light. These people were not the-- not the very nicest people. But-- so that's how I learned my English. Then I went to London. And in London, I was able to meet other people in the same situation. All-- all worried abut their loved ones, and they didn't know whether they were deported or what happened to them. Many just said they didn't get any mail from them from Germany. And-- and-- we-- but and we heard-- in England, we heard many times that people were deported, and there were-- we always heard that they were letting them starve. They're starving. And that drove me out of my mind, thinking that they're starving. I didn't know how to help them. I had no money myself, and I had-- didn't know how to help them. And since we didn't hear anything more from them, we didn't know where to-- where to reach them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942, my husband had gone to England on-- on some kind of a visa. He was not allowed to work, but when war broke out, he was given the choice of joining the army or being interned. And he right away didn't have to think. He joined the army, because he wanted to fight against the Germans. And he was in the army, and I met him as a soldier. A British soldier. My husband comes from Germany, from Berlin. He's German also. He is-- he had a brother who died only a few years ago, but both his parents were also deported to Riga. And I met him there. We got married. We had nothing, no money, no nothing. But we had a license, and we went to a synagogue that was completely bombed out. And we were there for 10 minutes. All I had was a little black dress. We had no party or anything, but we got married in the synagogue and-- and by a Rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's--

speaker: interviewer
text: His birth name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. My husband's name is Rudy, and his name when we got married was Kleczewski-- K-L-E-C-Z-E-W-S-K-I. His parents had come from Lodz. They were also Polish Jews living in Berlin, and they had come from Lodz. And his father was a tailor. His mother's name was Hedwig. Nee-- her maiden name was Fleischmann-- F-L-E-I-S-C-H-M-A-N-N. He-- he was a tailor, and he had a tailor place in Berlin, and my husband came to-- to London in 1939. His brother had immigrated to Montevideo in 1938. He had only about 10 years ago or so, he came to Israel, and he died just about four years ago, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband have a trade when he came to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: He learned to be a trotter in Germany. He loved horses, and he became a trotter. He-- he learned that profession. And he thought-- his father thought that maybe-- he always heard about racing in America. Everybody was thinking of America, not everybody was able to get into America. But that was the wish for everybody, to get to America. That was the country everybody wanted to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- his father thought that-- he heard so much about horse racing, and he thought it would be a good profession for him. And he did learn it, and he became a trotter, but he couldn't do anything with it here in America. What did he work as here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he worked mostly as a-- a-- as-- as cook. Because in the army, he cooked. He was a cook. He became a cook, and then he-- that's-- he took up that profession. He was mostly a cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out, after the war, about your family, how everybody had fared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When the war was over, in-- as soon as the war was over, we had contact again. We contacted my sister in France and my sister in Shanghai. We started a correspondence, and my sister in France wanted first for me to come over to her. My sister in France was-- and her husband and son, and also her husband's parents, were all hiding. And they were hiding-- they were hiding, and a priest was hiding them in his house, a priest that they had befriended. And he saved their life, because they had-- for many-- many times they were almost deported, and somehow, he was able to save their life. He was hiding them in the forest, and he-- the-- my nephew, their son, who was then a small-- who was then a child, he was hidden on various farms that the priest took care of. He was previously, not too long ago, honored by Yad Vashem. And he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very fine priest. I don't know, at this point, his name. But he lives in-- in-- I forgot the name of the city now. In the south of France. Clermont-Ferrand. It's called Clermont-Ferrand, and he lives there, and he's close to 100 years old. And he and his parents, who, of course, have been-- have died a long time ago, were all given some kind of a medal for saving various families. So this is why my sister in France is alive. My sister in Shanghai also went through a lot, because Shanghai was-- had a terrible climate, and that both her-- she and her husband became quite ill. They came to the United States in 1947. I-- I now visited my sister in France. She wanted very much to get together. And while I was in there-- I-- I was always terribly worried about my family, but I never thought for one moment that they would not be here anymore. For some reason, I did not believe that is possible. I always-- I was always so concerned about their suffering, that they didn't have enough food, that they didn't have-- they weren't warm enough. But it never came to my mind that they-- that they wouldn't be around. That anything really serious could happen. But when I to-- came to my sister in France, and she was-- she had her family, her parents-in-law, I realized by the way they talked that they have perished. And that gave me-- that-- indescribable. I became so ill. I had such a-- I had a nervous breakdown. I came back, and I was quite ill for a long time, because I did not believe that they had actually perished. And then I learned that somehow-- they have learned-- I really don't know, but it was obvious or what. This was now a year after the war was over, and we were all looking for our parents, like everybody else did. There's newspapers, there was this Aufbau that had all the people that were looking for each other, and you know, some people found their family members, but I never read anything about my parents. I-- we con-- we all, the three of us contacted many agencies who had names of those who had been to concentration camps, who had-- who were deported. But my parents and my sister and brother's name never came up, so somehow my sister-- she-- she knew that there were no more. I didn't. And that was very, very hard to take.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever had any verification at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Really not. We don't have no idea what happened to them after the Warsaw Ghetto. We don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you stayed in England at this point? You remained in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we-- we remained in England until 1948. We decided then and there that-- that both our parents, my husband's parents and mine, always wanted to come to the United States, and my husband was released from-- from-- from the army. And there was really not that much future in England. Times were-- at the times, it was very hard. There was very little food in England. Very hard to find jobs. And we decided to go to America. And we came to America in 1948. And my sister, as I mentioned before, came from Shanghai to America in 1947. And my sister in France is still in France, still lives in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you settle in the-- in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came here, the first thing, we went to-- my sister landed in San Francisco from China. She landed in San Fran-- and we couldn't wait to get together. So the very first thing, three weeks after we came to New York, we went to San Francisco by train, three nights and three days. And-- and we stayed in San Francisco for a year, but we couldn't make a living. The next thing, when we came to America, was we must make a living. We have to-- we have to make a decent living so that we can get some kind of a place to stay. So after a year, we came back to New York because we realized in the first three weeks that we were here that New York is the place where you can make a living. And we came back to New York, and my husband and I went to a hotel in the Catskills. And I cleaned 26 rooms [CHUCKLES] every day and six bathrooms. And we got a little money together, and then we were able to get an apartment. And all five of us-- my sister, meanwhile, had a little baby-- and all five of us moved into this apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you make a living after going through the-- the domestic end of it? How did you make a living for most of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that-- yes, after the domestics, in time. Then I took-- I didn't go to college, but I did take-- I attended many seminars. And I decided that I-- what I-- I always love to read, and I decided that what I would like to be is a librarian. And I attended many seminars. I read a lot of books, and I was able to land a job. At first, I-- I-- I worked in a library in-- not in New York, but meanwhile, we moved to Rockland County, to Spring Valley. In New York-- yes, I'm sorry. In New York, I-- my son went to school. I-- we have a son. His name is Allen Clifford-- A-L-L-E-N Clifford. And he-- when he started school, I worked as a volunteer in that library, school library. This is what made me-- what-- it made me come that interest to become a librarian. And I took a lot of seminars, and then we moved to Rockland County. And in Rockland County, I became a librarian in a large company, for which I worked 21 years. I created a library at that company.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was Allen born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Allen was born in 1957. And he is now married. His wife's name is Ellen, and her maiden name is Menachem. [CHUCKLES] And they have-- they have two children. Of course, darling children. My granddaughter, her name is Lauren, L-A-U-R-E-N, and she's now nine years old. And my grandson is Justin, and he's seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the things that you have done that you are most proud of? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Besides Allen and the grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I am proud of the fact that we were able to-- that I was able to pull out of the tragic situation of losing a-- a family. It was-- I-- I am not going to say that I pulled out completely. No way that I pulled out completely. There is no way that I can ever forget anything like that. It's very hard when holidays come, when Passover comes, and the high holidays. There isn't a holiday that I don't miss my family. My father used to give such a wonderful Seder. My brother helped him, and-- and they would sing, and it was-- and there is just no holiday that I don't-- there isn't a day that I don't think of them. But I am proud of the fact that I was able to pull through. I have helped a lot of people along the way. I've helped my sister, who lives here, who-- this sister, Mary, who used to live in Shanghai. I have-- my sister who is in France is now quite elderly, and we have helped her come over here many times. And-- and I've-- we've-- I've helped a lot of people along the way, and I think I'm proud of that. I belong to organizations, such as other Holocaust Centers, and I helped other survivors survive like I did. And we do a lot for other people. We belong to the Meals on Wheels. We belong to various organizations. We go every once in a while, and we bring the meals to-- to people who can't get out. And I am very active with other Holocausts Centers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there some message or some closing statement you would like to make for people to remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think the closing statement that I would like to make is that I learned in life that there is nothing more important-- no money, nothing will replace a family. A family member is everything-- is the happiest thing that you can have. And if I can be given anything at all, if somebody would ask me what is it that you want, anything that would make me happy? And I would only say family. And I would always urge those people that have family to appreciate-- to appreciate one another and not hate. This one thing that I want everybody to know. This hatred that goes on amongst people, amongst religions, amongst-- I don't understand why people hate. If you have family, if you have children, you have parents, you have grandparents, it's such a wonderful thing to have family. It is-- nothing can replace a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I kind of hope and pray that nothing like this will ever happen. Because this was all out of hatred. The question I always asked, why did you hate me? To kill my family, most of my family, and I haven't gotten the answer to that yet. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is my grandmother, my mother's mother, whom I am named after. Her name was Esther Pearl, and her maiden name was Eiba. And this picture was taken in Poland, in the late 1800s. This is my parents' wedding invitation, which was taken in the beginning of 1900 in Warsaw, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] This is a picture of my parents. The store that my father owned. It was taken in Frankfurt around 1923 or '24. To the very right is my father, my mother, and I am holding my mother's hand. To the left is-- in front is my sister Mary, behind her, my sister Regina. Next to Regina is a cousin. Her name was Rosa, who had visited us at that time. In front of her is my brother Leo and my sister Rosie. This is the Bar Mitzvah invitation for my brother Leo. It was taken-- his Bar Mitzvah was on Saturday, March the 14th, 1931 in Frankfurt. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] These are the people who perished in my immediate family. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] These are my husband's parents, Max and Hedwig Kleczewski, and the picture was taken in Berlin about 1939. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] These are my parents, Abraham and Selda Abie, and this picture was taken in Frankfurt shortly before they were deported in 1938. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is my brother Leo and my sister Rosa, and the picture was taken about 1935 on an outing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- [INAUDIBLE] I think that they went to the town that-- it-- it-- this was taken in Frankfurt, near Frankfurt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my husband, Rudi. The picture was taken in-- during-- about 1944, while he was in the British Army.
